statusLine: EU fuel security is under pressure with 21 of 27 countries holding critical-level reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall averages near mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security shows significant strain across multiple countries and fuel types. While EU-wide averages remain close to the 90-day mandatory minimum (petrol 94.5 days, diesel 95.8 days, jet fuel 89.9 days), the distribution reveals concerning vulnerabilities. A total of 21 of 27 EU countries are holding critical-level reserves in at least one fuel category, with some nations showing severe shortfalls across multiple fuel types.

The most concerning situations are in Romania, Poland, and Spain, where all three fuel types are at critical levels. Romania faces the most severe shortage with diesel reserves at just 20.2 days—far below the 90-day requirement. Eastern European countries appear particularly vulnerable, with Poland, Lithuania, Latvia, and Slovakia all showing multiple critical-level fuel categories. Even major economies like Germany, France, and Italy are experiencing critical shortages, primarily in diesel and jet fuel.

Jet fuel emerges as the most constrained fuel type across the EU, with the 89.9-day average falling below the mandatory minimum. This shortage affects aviation hubs and reflects broader supply chain disruptions. The current Brent crude price of $96.24 per barrel, up 1.57% and approximately 27% higher since the Middle East conflict began, suggests continued upward pressure on fuel costs and potential supply constraints.
keyPoints: 21 of 27 EU countries have critical-level reserves in at least one fuel type, indicating widespread strain
Romania faces the most severe shortage with diesel at just 20.2 days, well below the 90-day mandatory minimum
Jet fuel shows the tightest supply across the EU at 89.9 days average, falling below required levels
Major economies including Germany, France, and Italy are experiencing critical shortages in diesel and jet fuel
Brent crude at $96.24 per barrel reflects ongoing supply pressure, up 27% since the Middle East conflict began
